definition: A state where fiscal dynamics partially override monetary policy as the dominant macro force, reflected in maturity-dependent correlation bifurcation. The front end still transmits monetary policy intent (negative 2Y-SPX) while the back end transmits fiscal supply pressure (positive 30Y-SPX). Full fiscal dominance would be signaled by 2Y-SPX turning positive.
evidence: Bianchi-Melosi fiscal theory extension of NK model provides theoretical foundation
2Y-SPX negative = monetary credibility; 30Y-SPX positive = fiscal reality
US at ~6.5% deficit/GDP with ~125% debt/GDP and no credible consolidation path
Fiscal dominance makes CB reaction function endogenous (challenger_01)
Falsifiable prediction: 2Y-SPX turning positive signals transition to full fiscal dominance
Maturity bifurcation documented by six independent agents
Bianchi-Melosi fiscal theory extension of NK model formalizes the mechanism
Fiscal dominance makes the Taylor Rule reaction function endogenous
$2T+ annual issuance with no consolidation path is the structural driver
$36T+ debt with net interest ~$1T/year approaching post-WWII maximum
0/0 base rate for FFR exceeding 5.5% with debt/GDP >100%
Pause at 5.25-5.50% with core PCE at 4.3% is observable revealed preference
UK Truss episode (2022) provides quasi-experimental confirmation of market-imposed fiscal discipline
Kalecki channel recycles 10-25% of rate-hike contractionary intent as fiscal stimulus to bondholders
open_questions: What are the precise conditions for transition from partial to full fiscal dominance?
Is partial fiscal dominance reversible without fiscal consolidation?
How does debt/GDP trajectory interact with the degree of fiscal dominance?
At what point does interest expense as % of revenue make fiscal dominance self-reinforcing?
What specific conditions would trigger the transition from partial to full fiscal dominance?
Is 2Y-SPX turning positive a reliable signal or could it reflect other dynamics?
At what debt/GDP ratio does fiscal dominance become irreversible?
Is the binding constraint structural (fiscal arithmetic) or political (appointment capture, mandate erosion)?
What are the relative weights of structural vs political drivers?
Does the Truss analogue apply to the US given reserve currency status?
